questions.question: Explain two advantages of using NETCONF-YANG over SNMP for IoT device management. (4 marks)
keyPoints: Better support for complex configurations through structured YANG data models versus limited SNMP MIBs.
Superior security with mandatory SSH transport and built-in authentication versus weak community strings in SNMPv1/v2c.
Transaction support with atomic commit/rollback preventing inconsistent device states.

questions.question: Describe the NETCONF protocol layers and their functions in IoT management systems. (10 marks)
keyPoints: Transport Layer: Provides secure communication (SSH mandatory) between client and server.
RPC Layer: Remote Procedure Call mechanism for request-reply exchanges using <rpc> and <rpc-reply> elements.
Operations Layer: Defines base protocol operations (<get-config>, <edit-config>, <copy-config>, <lock>, <unlock>, etc.).
Content Layer: Contains configuration and state data in structured XML format, defined by YANG models.
Application to IoT: Each layer addresses specific IoT management challenges from secure transport to structured device configuration.

questions.question: Differentiate between 'config true' and 'config false' in YANG modeling. Give an example of each in an IoT context. (4 marks)
keyPoints: 'config true' (default) means the data node represents configurable data that can be modified via NETCONF operations.
'config false' means the data node represents read-only operational state data that cannot be changed by the manager.
Example config true: sampling-interval (admin can change how often a sensor reads).
Example config false: current temperature reading (read-only runtime value from the sensor).

questions.question: Explain the NETCONF-YANG architecture for managing IoT devices in a smart city deployment. Describe the components and the steps involved. (10 marks)
keyPoints: Components: Management Application (NMS), NETCONF Server (Controller), IoT Devices (with NETCONF agents), YANG Models, Configuration Database.
Step 1 - Discovery: Devices exchange <hello> messages with the server to advertise YANG capabilities.
Step 2 - Configuration: Server pushes configs to sensors using <edit-config> operations.
Step 3 - Monitoring: Operational data retrieved via <get> operations to monitor sensor status.
Step 4 - Automated Response: Threshold violations trigger automatic reconfiguration or alerts.
Step 5 - Maintenance: Firmware and policy changes deployed transactionally across device groups.
